text: Upon entry into the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes a complete obliteration of the posterior cul-de-sac, indicating dense adhesions in the pelvic region.
The surgeon begins the procedure by carefully removing filmy adhesions that tether the sigmoid colon to the left pelvic side wall, using laparoscopic instruments to gently separate the tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection cranially to mobilize the sigmoid colon, separating it from surrounding structures for improved access.
The ureter is visually identified at the level of the pelvic brim during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection down into the pelvis, revealing the ureter at the level of the ovary, which is tethered by adhesions.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to separate the left ovary from the underlying rectum, carefully freeing the ovary from adhesions.
The surgeon begins dissecting the rectum away from the posterior aspect of the uterus, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to mobilize the ovary out of the posterior cul-de-sac, freeing it from surrounding adhesions.
The surgeon temporarily sutures the left ovary to the round ligament, effectively retracting it out of the surgical field.
The surgeon bluntly mobilizes the right ovary from the pelvic sidewall, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter transperitoneally, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon enters the retroperitoneum just below the level of the right ureter, facilitating its complete dissection along the right pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully detach rectal attachments from the posterior uterus. The first attachment is freed, improving visualization of the rectum. Dissection continues laterally to release the nodule from attachments on both side walls.
The surgeon carefully dissects the nodule from the posterior surface of the uterus, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses electrosurgery at the interface between the nodule and the posterior uterus, carefully applying energy to separate the nodule from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon mobilizes the mass off the right pelvic side wall, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to ensure complete dissection.
A hand is placed in the vagina to assist in delineating healthy vaginal tissue from the nodule, providing clear visual differentiation between the structures.
The surgeon temporarily tethers the right ovary out of the way, ensuring clear access to the surgical field for continued dissection.
The surgeon carefully removes the peritoneum overlying the right pelvic sidewall, previously tethered to the ovary, ensuring the ureter is avoided during the procedure.
The surgeon inserts a rectal probe into the patient's rectum, likely to assist in delineating anatomical structures during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the endometriosis nodule from its lateral attachments on the right pelvic sidewall, carefully dissecting the tissue to free the nodule.
The surgeon carefully frees the endometriosis nodule from its attachments to the rectum, using precise dissection techniques to separate the nodule from the surrounding rectal tissue.
The surgeon observes residual endometriosis nodule remaining after careful dissection, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully grasps the residual nodule with a tenaculum and dissects it off the rectum using cold scissors, ensuring precise separation from the rectal tissue.
The surgeon exposes a friable area of the muscularis layer following the removal of the endometriosis nodule from its attachments.
The surgeon oversews the exposed area in multiple layers, ensuring the rectal lumen remains unobstructed.
An EEA sizer is passed through the rectum to test the patency and integrity of the lumen after the first layer of oversewing.
A barbed PDO suture is visible being used in the surgical field, likely for securing or closing tissue layers.
The surgeon excises the peritoneum overlying the left pelvic side wall, mirroring the procedure performed on the right side.
The surgeon completes the excision and performs a rectal leak test, checking for any signs of leakage in the rectal area.
